title: Sorting
summary: Arrange data in a defined order — essential for table sorting, ranked feeds, and priority-based UI rendering.
overview: Sorting algorithms order a collection by a comparator. JavaScript's Array.sort() is O(n log n) and uses a stable sort (TimSort). Understanding comparison functions, stable vs unstable sorts, and custom key extraction is more practically relevant for frontend engineers than implementing quicksort from scratch.
coreConcept: A comparator function (a, b) => number returns negative (a before b), zero (equal), or positive (b before a). Stable sort preserves relative order of equal elements — critical for multi-key sorting. Custom key extraction (Schwartzian transform) avoids recomputing expensive keys on every comparison.
problemPatterns: Rank items by score with tiebreakers
Custom stable sort with multiple criteria
signals: Data must be displayed in a user-defined order
Performance is important for large lists

frontendScenarios.title: Multi-Column Table Sorting
frontendScenarios.description: A data table lets users sort by any column, with secondary sort preserving previous order. Stable sort makes this work: sort by secondary key first, then by primary key.

frontendScenarios.title: Priority Feed Ranking
frontendScenarios.description: A social feed sorts posts by: pinned first, then by engagement score descending, then by recency descending. Three-level comparator.

dataThinking: In frontend, sorting is usually about comparator design, not algorithm choice. The browser's sort is fast enough. Your challenge is: (1) correct multi-key comparators, (2) stable sort semantics for progressive refinement, (3) extracting sort keys efficiently (precompute expensive keys before sorting).

naiveApproach.description: Bubble sort or selection sort — O(n²). Readable but impractical for large data.

betterApproach.description: Use Array.prototype.sort() with a custom comparator. For expensive key extraction, use the Schwartzian transform: map to [key, item], sort by key, map back to items.
betterApproach.complexity: O(n log n) time, O(n) space

visualizationSteps.label: Unsorted feed items
visualizationSteps.explanation: Three items: one pinned (low score), two regular. We want: pinned first, then by score.

visualizationSteps.label: After sort by pinned first
visualizationSteps.explanation: Item 2 (pinned) rises to top regardless of score. Regular items sorted by score descending.

visualizationSteps.label: Comparator logic

state.comparator: if pinned differs: pinned first. else: higher score first. else: newer first.

visualizationSteps.explanation: The comparator is a priority chain. Each level only applies when higher-priority keys are equal.

practiceItems.title: Table Column Sort with Toggle
practiceItems.question: Implement a useSortedData hook that takes an array and manages sort state (column key + direction). Clicking the same column toggles asc/desc; clicking a new column starts asc.
practiceItems.hint1: Store { key: string, dir: 'asc'|'desc' } as sort state. useMemo the sorted array.
practiceItems.hint2: When setting key: if same key, toggle dir. If new key, set dir to 'asc'.

practiceItems.title: Top-K Elements
practiceItems.question: Given an array of products with scores, return the top K products without sorting the entire array. This is more efficient when K is much smaller than N.
practiceItems.hint1: You don't need to sort everything — partial sort or a min-heap of size K works. For simplicity, sort descending and slice.
practiceItems.hint2: For the optimal O(n log k) solution, maintain a min-heap of size K. If a new item beats the heap minimum, replace it.

reflectionPrompts: JavaScript's Array.sort() became stable in V8 v7.0 (Node 11+). Why does sort stability matter for multi-column table sorting?
What is the Schwartzian transform and when would you use it in a React application?
When would you implement a custom sort algorithm rather than using the built-in Array.sort()?
How does sorting interact with React's key reconciliation when rendering sorted lists?
